title: Pretend Play Coffee Cart with Espresso Machine
summary: A kid-sized toy coffee cart with a shelf and dowel legs, paired with a jigsawed toy espresso machine complete with a dowel handle, knobs, and steam wand.
description:
The cart is a straightforward small case: short dowel legs screwed to the bottom shelf, sides and a center divider panel added with nails and glue, then a second shelf and the countertop stacked on top — the center panel is what reinforces the whole thing, so the earlier joints don't need to be perfect on their own. **The espresso machine is the detail piece that sells the toy** — its two side panels are cut identically with a jigsaw, then boxed in with a front, back, and top, all nailed and glued.

**Every control on the machine is a turned dowel, not a purchased knob** — a short section of 2" dowel becomes the espresso cup holder, a length of 3/4" dowel becomes the handle, two short 2" dowel discs become the control knobs, and a longer 3/4" dowel becomes the steam wand. Sand the machine smooth after the jigsaw cuts, since this is a toy meant for small hands to actually play with.

tools.note: For cutting the espresso machine sides.

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: From the 10 ft 1x12 cut the cart bottom 25 1/2", two sides 17 1/2", the shelf 24", and the top 32 1/2" — that is 117" of the 120" board, so cut longest first and do not waste an end. Cut the 24" centre panel from the 1x10 and the espresso machine sides 9 1/2" and front and back 12" from the 1x8. From the 1x2 cut the cart top support 26 1/2", the machine top 13 1/2", and two small trim pieces 2"; from the 1x3 cut the narrow detail board 12" and two small blocks 3 1/2". Cut four 3" legs, one 1 1/2" cup holder and two 1/2" knobs from the 2" dowel, and a 5" handle and 6" steam wand from the 3/4" dowel. Two parts use their whole board with nothing to spare — the 24" centre panel is the entire 2 ft 1x10 and the 12" wide detail board the entire 1 ft 1x4 — so if either factory end is not square you will need a little extra stock.

steps.title: Build the cart base
steps.body: Stand the 25 1/2" bottom board good-face-down and set a 3" dowel leg in each corner, held in far enough from the edges that the leg sits fully under the board. Predrill into the end of each dowel first — a screw driven into unpredrilled end grain will split it — then drive two 2" self-tapping screws down through the bottom into each leg. Two per leg, not one, or the leg will pivot the first time the cart is pushed. Stand the base on a flat floor and check it does not rock before anything else goes on.

steps.title: Attach the sides and center panel
steps.body: Set a 17 1/2" side upright on the bottom board at each end, outside faces flush with the ends of the 25 1/2" bottom. That leaves exactly 24" between the two sides, which is why the shelf and the centre panel are both 24" long — measure it before you fasten. Glue and drive 1 1/4" brads down through each side into the bottom, then stand the 24" centre panel between the sides midway along and nail it the same way. The centre panel is what reinforces the whole cart, so the earlier joints do not have to carry the load on their own.

steps.title: Add the shelf, support, and top
steps.body: Rest the 24" shelf on the centre panel and between the sides, check it level, and glue and nail through the sides into its ends. Run the 26 1/2" top support across the sides — it is 1" longer than the cart's 25 1/2" outside width, so let it project 1/2" at each end, or hold it in place and mark it to whatever your assembly actually measures. Then centre the 32 1/2" top on the cart: at 7" longer than the 25 1/2" bottom it overhangs 3 1/2" at each end, which is what gives the cart its counter. Glue and pin the top down with 1 1/4" brads into the sides, centre panel and top support.

steps.title: Cut the espresso machine sides
steps.body: Lay the machine's profile out on one 9 1/2" x 7 1/4" piece of 1x8 and cut it with the jigsaw, then trace that finished piece onto the second and cut the second slightly proud of the line. Clamp the two together and sand them as a pair down to the line so they end up genuinely identical — the box only closes cleanly if both sides match. Keep them stacked while you sand the sawn edges smooth; this is a toy that small hands will grip, so nothing should be left rough or sharp.

steps.title: Build the espresso machine box
steps.body: Assemble the box from the two shaped 9 1/2" sides and the two 12" front and back pieces, with the 13 1/2" 1x2 across the top. Note before you glue that the numbers do not quite close — 9 1/2" plus two 3/4" thicknesses is 11", not the 12" of the front and back — so dry-fit the four pieces and decide how you want the overhang to fall before committing. Glue every joint and pin with 1 1/4" brads driven from the faces you will not see. Check the front for square: the detail boards that go on next are all cut to 12", the same as the front, and will only sit flush if it is true.

steps.title: Add the detail boards and blocks
steps.body: Glue and pin the 12" wide detail board and the 12" narrow detail board across the front of the machine, edges flush with the front's own edges since both are cut to its full 12". Add the two 3 1/2" small blocks where the group head and drip tray go, and fit the 2" trim pieces between the full-length front and back rather than over them. Use 5/8" brads on the thinner pieces so nothing can point out of the back face, and glue everything — on a toy, glue is what holds and the brads only stop it sliding while the glue cures.

steps.title: Add the dowel details
steps.body: Cut the dowels on the miter saw with the stock clamped down or held in a V-block — a round dowel will roll into the blade if you hold it by hand. Glue the 1 1/2" length of 2" dowel to the front as the cup holder and the two 1/2" discs of the same dowel on as control knobs. For the 5" handle and the 6" steam wand, drill a shallow socket for each so the dowel seats into the wood instead of relying on a glue joint to end grain, which is the weakest joint there is. A child will pull on all four of these, so glue every one and let them cure fully before the toy goes into use.

steps.title: Finish
steps.body: Sand the whole cart and the machine, rounding every corner and easing every edge — this is handled by small hands, so nothing should be left sharp. Fill the brad holes, sand them flush, and vacuum the dust off. Prime and paint with a finish rated safe for children's furniture, and let it cure fully rather than just dry to the touch before the cart is played with.

images.alt: Pretend play coffee cart with a toy espresso machine
